learnings.title: Adopting an existing PR is a repeatable workflow, not an ad-hoc task
learnings.body: Over two weeks, 'adopt PR NNNN' came up ~15 times, always with the same steps: check the change is still necessary, update from trunk, fix CI, address open review threads, and sometimes split an oversized PR into independent changes (e.g. #66590 became three PRs). Nothing encodes this today, so every run rediscovers the steps and the pitfalls (diffs polluted by bad merges, stale review context).
learnings.author: paul

learnings.title: The autoresearch loop protocol is pasted verbatim into every run
learnings.body: Six-plus conversations started with the same long 'You are now in autoresearch mode' prompt defining the one-change-measure-report iteration protocol. The protocol belongs in a skill that takes only the brief and the metric, so improvements to the protocol propagate instead of forking per paste.
learnings.author: paul

learnings.title: The taxonomic filter's async-search races keep producing the same bug
learnings.body: Three separate fixes in two weeks addressed false 'No results' states in the taxonomic filter (stale responses racing fresh queries, tab-switch not refetching, react-window update loops). They are one class of bug: search state that doesn't tie results to the query that produced them. The invariant belongs in the modifying-taxonomic-filter skill so the next change doesn't reintroduce it.
learnings.author: paul

learnings.title: Most error-tracking signal work is noise reduction with recurring code patterns
learnings.body: A large share of inbox-driven fixes were 'stop capturing a benign exception': expected access denials, network flakes in fire-and-forget calls, third-party injected scripts, timeout signals that should propagate, and non-Error objects passed to captureException. The decision tree (swallow vs propagate vs normalize vs suppress-by-fingerprint) repeats and should be written down as a repo-side skill.
learnings.author: paul

learnings.title: Moving conventions from instructions to linters pays off immediately
learnings.body: The em-dash rule lived in AGENTS.md and was routinely violated; turning it into an automatic commit-time replacement removed the review churn entirely. Same shape as the StreamingHttpResponse wrapper enforcement. When a convention is checkable, spend the effort on the linter, not on better prose.
learnings.author: paul

learnings.title: The agent fleet itself needs observability
learnings.body: Recurring questions: what share of PRs does stamphog approve, how accurate are its denials, what does a PR cost per model, are shepherd runs looping on the same PR. Several conversations re-derived the same queries from scratch. A skill for assessing the fleet with AI observability and engineering analytics would make these one-liners.
learnings.author: paul

learnings.title: Bundle-size work has dedicated tooling that isn't documented as a skill
learnings.body: The eager-graph checker, lazy-loading of scene edges, the vendored paths-filter, and the CloudFront 10MB gzip ceiling on the toolbar bundle all came up repeatedly. The knowledge of how to read the eager-graph report and which lazy-load patterns are safe lives only in past conversations.
learnings.author: paul

learnings.title: CI/test performance is a continuous campaign with compounding wins
learnings.body: DB-free serializer validation tests, pytest boot-cost cuts, selective TransactionTestCase flushes, jest related-tests fan-out taming, and trunk.io flaky quarantine all landed in the same two weeks. Each win came from measuring first (slowest tests, hour-by-hour CI speed) and the measurement queries are worth encoding alongside fixing-flaky-tests.
learnings.author: paul

learnings.title: CI bots should share one updatable PR comment
learnings.body: Multiple bots each posting their own comment (bundle size, eager graph, hobby deploy, stamphog refusals) buried signal. Consolidating into a single shared report comment with a stable section order made PRs readable again; new CI reporters should join it rather than posting their own.
learnings.author: paul
